subsystem: Minetest/Luanti engine item-metadata layer. ItemStackMetadata::setString is the override that stores a single key/value string pair into an item stack's metadata store. ItemStackMetadata is a subclass of the generic Metadata key/value store, specialized for per-item data that is serialized into the item stack, persisted to the world save, and transmitted between server and clients. This particular method is the write-path entry point that mod code (via the Lua ItemStackMetaRef API) and engine code use to attach named string attributes to items, including the special 'tool_capabilities' attribute.
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified method name ItemStackMetadata::setString, the base class Metadata::setString, the TOOLCAP_KEY constant, the updateToolCapabilities() helper, and the sanitize_string() free function — this exact combination is the Minetest/Luanti src/itemstackmetadata.cpp file. The header comment `/* sample: primevul_validation_0144_fixed */` and the corpus filename train_0197_cwe74.cpp indicate this is the REMEDIATED variant of a PrimeVul sample tagged CWE-74 (injection). No SPDX or copyright header is present in the excerpt, but the symbol vocabulary is unambiguous for this codebase.
trust_boundary: Both parameters, `name` and `var`, are attacker-influenced. ItemStackMetadata::setString is reachable from the Lua modding API (a semi-trusted mod, or on many servers player-authored code) and, more importantly, the resulting metadata is serialized into a single flat byte string using in-band control-byte delimiters and then written to the network stream and the world database. Anything a player or mod can place into a name or value crosses the trust boundary the moment it is serialized, because the serializer has no length prefixes — it relies on delimiter bytes (historically \x01 start marker, \x02 key/value delimiter, \x03 pair delimiter) to structure the record. Therefore any delimiter byte embedded in `name` or `var` is hostile input that can restructure the serialized record. The sanitize_string() calls are exactly the guard that enforces this boundary; the base Metadata::setString and the downstream serializer trust that their inputs have already been cleaned.

what_the_code_does.code:
/* sample: primevul_validation_0144_fixed */
bool ItemStackMetadata::setString(const std::string &name, const std::string &var)
what_the_code_does.explanation: A corpus tag comment followed by the method signature. This is ItemStackMetadata's override of setString, taking a key `name` and a value `var`, both as const std::string references (so arbitrary binary content and arbitrary length are permitted — std::string is not NUL-terminated-limited). It returns a bool that propagates whether the underlying store actually changed. Because this overrides the generic Metadata::setString, it is the single choke point through which named item attributes are written, which is why the sanitization belongs here rather than deeper in the store.

what_the_code_does.code:
std::string clean_name = name;
	std::string clean_var = var;
what_the_code_does.explanation: The method takes private, mutable copies of both inputs. The originals are const references and must not be mutated (they may be caller-owned), so copies are made to receive the in-place sanitization on the next two lines. This also means the caller's strings are left untouched, and only the copies flow forward into the store.

The security-critical step. sanitize_string() is called by-reference on each copy to strip the serialization control bytes (the delimiter characters used by the ItemStackMetadata serializer) out of both the key and the value. This is the defense that makes the flat, delimiter-based serialization format injection-safe: after this runs, neither string can contain a byte that the deserializer would interpret as a field boundary. The fact that BOTH name and value are sanitized (not just the value) matters, because either side of a key/value pair can be used to forge an extra pair boundary.

Delegates the actual storage to the base class Metadata::setString using the already-sanitized key and value. The base class performs the map insert/erase and returns whether the stored state changed (typically true on a real change, false if setting an identical value or a no-op erase). That return is captured in `result` to be handed back to the caller. From here on the value lives in the metadata map and will later be serialized with the delimiter scheme — which is safe precisely because the delimiters were removed above.

Special-cases the tool-capabilities attribute. TOOLCAP_KEY is a named constant (the reserved key, 'tool_capabilities', used to store a serialized ToolCapabilities structure on an item). When that specific key is written, updateToolCapabilities() is invoked to re-parse the stored value and refresh the cached, deserialized ToolCapabilities object on the item stack so that damage/dig behavior reflects the new value immediately. Note the comparison is against clean_name (the sanitized key), which is correct: a legitimate 'tool_capabilities' key contains no control bytes and so survives sanitization unchanged and still matches.

Returns the change flag captured from the base-class store to the caller (e.g. the Lua binding uses it to report whether the set had effect). Note the return value reflects only the base store's result and is unaffected by the sanitization or the tool-capability refresh — a value that was silently altered by sanitize_string() is still reported the same as an untouched one.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the whole security contract of the fix and it is only as strong as sanitize_string()'s completeness — which is not shown here. The ItemStackMetadata serializer packs all key/value pairs into one flat byte string separated by in-band control bytes (historically \x01 as the format/start marker, \x02 as the key/value separator, and \x03 as the pair separator). If sanitize_string() fails to strip EVERY byte the deserializer treats as structural (e.g. it removes the pair delimiter but not the start marker, or a newer format byte was added to the serializer without being added to the sanitizer), then an attacker who controls `name` or `var` can embed that byte and forge additional key/value pairs during deserialization — classic CWE-74 injection into a serialization stream. The impact is metadata spoofing: injecting a forged 'tool_capabilities' pair to gain out-of-spec dig speed / infinite tool durability, or overwriting other reserved keys, on any client/server that deserializes the record. The invariant the implementation must uphold: the set of bytes stripped by sanitize_string() must be a superset of every byte with structural meaning in the serializer, and the two must be kept in lockstep forever.

When the key is 'tool_capabilities', updateToolCapabilities() (not shown) re-parses the attacker-controlled value string into a ToolCapabilities object. sanitize_string() only removes the serialization delimiter bytes; it does NOT validate that the value is a well-formed, in-range ToolCapabilities encoding. So the value that reaches the tool-capability deserializer is still fully attacker-controlled apart from the stripped delimiters. Any bounds/format weakness in that deserializer (unchecked counts, unbounded map sizes, out-of-range enum/level values, integer handling) is reachable directly from setString on the tool_capabilities key — a latent CWE-20 / parser-robustness concern living in the implementation this snippet only invokes. The invariant: updateToolCapabilities() must fully validate the deserialized structure and fail closed on malformed input, never trusting that a sanitized string is a valid ToolCapabilities blob.

Silent, lossy mutation of the key is a semantic/integrity hazard even when it is not directly exploitable. If a caller sets a name that contains a control byte, sanitize_string() rewrites the key, so the pair is stored under a DIFFERENT key than requested (or two distinct requested keys can collapse to the same stored key after stripping), and the bool returned on line 12 does not signal that the key was altered. Downstream logic that later does getString(originalName) will miss it, and the collision case lets one attacker-chosen key silently overwrite another. This is not memory-unsafe, but it is a correctness/authorization surprise: a mod cannot tell that its key was transformed, and last-writer-wins collisions across sanitized keys can clobber previously stored data. The invariant: callers must treat the stored key as the sanitized form, and ideally the API should reject rather than silently rewrite keys that change under sanitization.

Ordering correctness: sanitization happens BEFORE the base-class store and before the TOOLCAP_KEY branch, which is the safe order — the store and the tool-capability refresh only ever see cleaned data. The residual risk is architectural rather than in this line: because Metadata::setString is a virtual base entry point, any OTHER code path that inserts into the same metadata map without routing through this override (e.g. a bulk deserialize-then-store path, or a direct base-class call) would bypass sanitize_string() entirely and reintroduce the injection. The invariant: every write into the item metadata map that will later be re-serialized must pass through this sanitizing override; no back door may write raw strings into the store.

summary: This is the remediated ("_fixed") variant of a CWE-74 injection in Minetest/Luanti ItemStackMetadata::setString: it now copies and runs sanitize_string() on both the key and value before storing them, stripping the in-band control-byte delimiters used by the delimiter-based metadata serializer so an attacker cannot forge extra key/value pairs. The code is low residual risk, but its safety rests entirely on sanitize_string() (not shown) stripping every structural byte the serializer uses, on updateToolCapabilities() safely parsing the still-attacker-controlled tool_capabilities value, and on all metadata writes routing through this sanitizing override.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Sole write-path choke point for item metadata; both parameters are attacker-influenced and become serialized in-band, so the method carries the injection-prevention contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: std::string clean_name = name; / std::string clean_var = var;
reviewed_anchors.reason: Copies exist to receive in-place sanitization; the silent lossy rewrite of the key (collision / altered-key) is a correctness/integrity concern worth a low finding.
The entire security fix; completeness vs. the serializer's delimiter set is the central latent contract (CWE-74).
Base store is invoked only with sanitized data here, but any alternate write path bypassing this override would reintroduce injection — architectural invariant.

A reserved key literal compared against the sanitized key; a legitimate 'tool_capabilities' contains no control bytes so it survives sanitization and matches correctly — no security contract of its own.
Re-parses the still-attacker-controlled tool_capabilities value; sanitization does not validate structure, so a latent parser-robustness (CWE-20) finding attaches here.
Pass-through change flag from the base store; carries no direct security contract, though it fails to signal that a key was altered by sanitization (captured under the lossy-mutation finding).

findings.title: Injection safety depends entirely on sanitize_string() covering every serializer delimiter byte
findings.bug_class: Serialization/format-string injection into a delimiter-based record
findings.invariant: sanitize_string() must strip every byte that the ItemStackMetadata serializer/deserializer treats as structural (start marker, key/value delimiter, pair delimiter, and any format-version byte), and must stay in lockstep with the serializer if new delimiters are ever added.
findings.failure_mode: If sanitize_string() misses any structural byte, an attacker who controls a metadata key or value (via the Lua ItemStackMetaRef API or any player action that writes item metadata) embeds that byte; on deserialization it is interpreted as a field boundary, forging extra key/value pairs — e.g. injecting or overwriting the 'tool_capabilities' pair to grant illegitimate tool power/durability, or overwriting other reserved keys. This is the exact bug the '_fixed' suffix remediates; the residual risk is incompleteness of the sanitizer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read sanitize_string() and the ItemStackMetadata (de)serialize routines together; confirm the stripped byte set is a superset of every structural byte the format uses, including the format/start marker and any version byte, with a test that round-trips keys/values containing each delimiter.

findings.title: updateToolCapabilities() parses still-attacker-controlled value without structural validation by the sanitizer
findings.bug_class: Deserialization of untrusted input / missing input validation
findings.invariant: updateToolCapabilities() must fully validate the deserialized ToolCapabilities structure (counts, map sizes, enum/level ranges, numeric bounds) and fail closed on malformed input; sanitize_string() removing delimiter bytes does NOT make the value a well-formed capabilities blob.
findings.failure_mode: Writing the 'tool_capabilities' key drives an immediate re-parse of the attacker-supplied value. Since only delimiter bytes are stripped, the value is otherwise fully attacker-controlled, so any unchecked count/size/range in the ToolCapabilities deserializer is reachable directly from setString, potentially causing over-allocation, out-of-range logic, or malformed cached state used in damage/dig calculations.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect updateToolCapabilities() and the ToolCapabilities deserializer for bounds checks on group counts, level/time-array sizes, and numeric ranges; confirm it rejects malformed blobs rather than trusting the sanitized string.

findings.title: Silent lossy rewrite of the metadata key can collide/clobber and is not signalled to the caller
findings.bug_class: Silent input transformation causing key collision / data integrity surprise
findings.invariant: Either the API must reject keys that change under sanitization, or all readers must query using the sanitized key form; the returned bool should not imply the key was stored verbatim.
findings.failure_mode: A key containing control bytes is silently rewritten, so it is stored under a different key than requested and later getString(originalName) misses it; two distinct requested keys that differ only in stripped bytes collapse to the same stored key, letting one silently overwrite the other. The bool on line 12 reflects only the base store's change flag and gives no indication the key was altered, so callers cannot detect the transformation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any caller relies on retrieving metadata by the original (unsanitized) key, and whether key collisions after sanitization can clobber security-relevant reserved keys.

findings.title: Sanitizing override must be the only path that writes re-serialized metadata
findings.invariant: Every write into the item-metadata map that will subsequently be serialized must route through this sanitizing ItemStackMetadata::setString override; no code may insert raw, unsanitized strings via the base Metadata store or a bulk deserialize path.
findings.failure_mode: If any other code path (a direct Metadata::setString call, a copy/merge helper, or a deserialize-then-store routine) inserts strings without sanitization, delimiter bytes re-enter the store and the injection is reintroduced despite this fix being present.
findings.what_to_confirm: Grep the codebase for other callers of the base Metadata::setString and for deserialize paths that populate ItemStackMetadata; ensure none bypass sanitize_string().

audit_checklist: Read sanitize_string() and confirm it strips every structural byte of the ItemStackMetadata serialization format (start/format marker, key/value delimiter, pair delimiter, any version byte) — not just a subset.
Verify the sanitizer and the serializer/deserializer are maintained together, with a round-trip test injecting each delimiter byte into keys and values.
Audit updateToolCapabilities() and the ToolCapabilities deserializer for bounds/range/count validation and fail-closed behavior on malformed attacker-supplied values.
Confirm no other code path writes into the item metadata map without passing through this sanitizing override (direct base Metadata::setString calls, merge/copy helpers, bulk deserialize).
Decide whether silently rewriting a key under sanitization is acceptable, or whether the API should reject such keys; check for key-collision clobbering of reserved keys.
Confirm the comparison on line 10 uses the sanitized key (clean_name) so the tool-capability refresh cannot be evaded or spoofed by delimiter bytes in the key.
